metadata.title: Prove It Scales: The Honest Stress Test
metadata.playlist: Make It Smart — Scale Your Madison Intelligence
metadata.source: INFO 7375 Assignment 4 brief — Scale Your Thing & Add Intelligence

beats.narration_text: Hola — this is Liam. Episode 5 of 7. The scale test section of Assignment 4 penalizes fabricated numbers twenty points. The assignment says so directly. 'It should handle a thousand requests' with no supporting evidence earns zero. Actually running fifty requests on test data, timing them, and reporting what broke earns the points — even if what broke is 'the AI API rate-limited me at thirty.' The honest result is what gets graded. Today you run one request, then ten, then fifty — all on test data, not live data — time each batch, find the breaking point, compute the cost, and answer the twenty-four-seven question. Let's go.

props.segment: Prove It Scales: The Honest Stress Test
props.command: Run 1 → 10 → 50 requests on test data. Time each. Find the break point. Compute cost.
props.runningText: fabricated numbers = -20 pts; honest breaking points earn the grade…
output: Timing: total seconds at 1, 10, and 50 requests
Break point: what fails first and at what number
Cost: per 100 requests · estimated monthly at 1000/day

beats.narration_text: The scale test runs in three stages on test data. Test data means records from your A3 data file — not live API calls, not real user requests. You are testing the workflow's behavior, not collecting new data. Stage one: run the workflow on one record. Note the total time from start to finish. Note whether the AI node succeeded and what it returned. Stage two: run it on ten records. Note the total time, the per-record time, and whether any record failed. Stage three: run it on fifty records. Note the total time, the per-record average, and — critically — note the first failure. What failed first? At what record number? What was the error? This is the breaking point. The breaking point is not a failure in the assignment — it is the finding. A workflow that breaks at forty-seven records under rate limiting with a one-minute pause and a retry gives you a real answer about capacity. A workflow that 'successfully processed fifty records' with no failures and no timing data gives the grader nothing to verify.

props.artifactHeading: Three stages — on test data only
artifactLines: Stage 1 — 1 record: total time · AI succeeded? · output returned?
Stage 2 — 10 records: total time · per-record avg · any failures?
Stage 3 — 50 records: total time · per-record avg · FIRST FAILURE (what, at record N?).
Breaking point is the finding — not a failure in the assignment.
Test data: records from A3 data file. Not live API calls. Not real user requests.
props.sparkLine: 1. 10. 50. Find where it breaks. That's the result.

beats.narration_text: AI API calls cost money. Most pricing is per token — the amount of text you send and receive in each call. After running the scale test, look at your AI service dashboard and find the actual cost of the fifty runs. Divide by fifty to get cost per request. Then compute two numbers: cost per hundred requests, and estimated monthly cost at a rate of one thousand requests per day — multiply cost per request by one thousand, then by thirty. These numbers are not hypothetical — they are based on what you actually spent during the test. Write them down before the dashboard resets. The assignment requires these numbers because they are what a real deployment decision is based on. A workflow that costs twelve dollars per day at a thousand requests per day has a real cost ceiling. A workflow that claims to scale to any volume without a cost estimate is aspirational, not engineered. One more note: if any node in the workflow is connected to a tool that buys something, charges a card, or makes a real-money transaction, that is a hard stop — check with a human before running the test.

artifactLines: After 50 runs: check AI service dashboard for actual cost of the test.
Cost per request: total cost ÷ 50.
Cost per 100 requests: cost per request × 100.
Monthly at 1000/day: cost per request × 1000 × 30.
Write it down before the dashboard resets — these are the numbers the assignment requires.
HARD STOP: if any node buys something or charges a card, stop and ask a human first.
props.sparkLine: Actual cost from the dashboard. Not estimated. Not rounded.

beats.narration_text: The assignment asks one explicit question: could this workflow run twenty-four hours a day, seven days a week? Answer it honestly with a yes or no followed by a because. 'No, because the n8n process runs on my laptop, which I need to close. A production deployment would require a cloud server running n8n continuously.' Or: 'No, because the AI API rate limit is ten requests per minute, and running continuously at that rate would cost approximately X per month.' Or: 'Yes, in principle, if deployed on a cloud server — but the monthly cost estimate is Y, which exceeds a student budget.' The honest answer — including 'I don't know' for factors you cannot currently measure — is what the grader is looking for. An unqualified 'yes, it can run twenty-four-seven' with no evidence or cost estimate is marketing, not engineering. Collect all findings into scale_test_results.md: the timing table, the break point, cost per hundred requests, monthly cost at a thousand per day, and the twenty-four-seven answer.

props.artifactHeading: 24/7 question + what goes in the file
artifactLines: 24/7 question: Yes/No because [honest constraint — hardware, cost, rate limit, or deployment].
'Yes, it can run 24/7' with no evidence = -20 pts. 'No, because [reason]' = full points.
scale_test_results.md contents:
Timing table: 1 record · 10 records · 50 records (total time + per-record avg).
Break point: what failed first · at record N · what error.
Cost: per 100 requests · monthly at 1000/day.
24/7 answer: yes/no + because.
props.sparkLine: Honest answer. Real numbers. Named file. Full points.

beats.narration_text: Verdict for Prove It Scales. Fabricated numbers cost twenty points. 'It should handle a thousand requests' with no evidence earns zero. Run the test: one record, ten records, fifty records, all on test data from the A3 file. Time each stage. Note the first failure and at what record count. Compute cost per request from the actual dashboard number. Derive cost per hundred and monthly at a thousand per day. Answer the twenty-four-seven question with yes or no and a specific reason — hardware, cost, rate limit, or deployment requirement. Collect everything into scale_test_results.md. Honest breaking points earn the grade. No evidence of testing does not.

props.artifactTitle: Prove It Scales: The Honest Stress Test
artifactLines: Fabricated = -20 pts. 'Should handle 1000' with no evidence = 0.
Protocol: 1 → 10 → 50 records on test data. Time each. Note first failure.
Cost: from actual dashboard. Per request · per 100 · monthly at 1000/day.
24/7 question: yes/no + specific reason (hardware/cost/rate limit/deployment).
scale_test_results.md: timing table · break point · cost · 24/7 answer.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this into Claude to write the scale test report from your real measurements. The prompt reads: I ran an n8n workflow scale test. Here are my results. Stage one, one record: total time [X seconds], AI succeeded yes or no. Stage two, ten records: total time [X seconds], failures [number and what error]. Stage three, fifty records: total time [X seconds], first failure at record [N], error [description]. Cost from my AI service dashboard for the fifty-record run: [dollar amount]. Format this into scale_test_results.md with four sections: a timing table comparing the three stages with per-record averages, a breaking point analysis naming the constraint that limits scale, a cost section with cost per hundred requests and estimated monthly cost at one thousand requests per day, and a twenty-four-seven production-readiness section with an honest assessment of what it would take to run this continuously. That prompt turns your raw numbers into a complete, formatted report. Fill in every bracket with your real measurements before you run it — the report is only as honest as the numbers you give it.

props.segment: Prove It Scales: The Honest Stress Test
props.command: My scale test results: 1 record → [X sec], success [yes/no]. 10 records → [X sec], failures [N]. 50 records → [X sec], first failure at record [N], error [desc]. Cost for 50 runs: [$]. Format into scale_test_results.md: timing table · break point analysis · cost section (per 100 + monthly at 1000/day) · 24/7 production-readiness assessment.
props.runningText: fill in every bracket with real measurements before running — report is only as honest as the numbers…

beats.narration_text: Prove It Scales: The Honest Stress Test. Liam, in for Bear.
